source_title: On the distance, reddening and progenitor of V838 Mon
note: One progenitor interpretation suggesting an initial mass of about 65 solar masses; model-dependent and not consensus.

source_title: V838 Mon: light echo evolution and distance estimate
note: Discussion section cites Tylenda et al. (2004) claim that progenitor was most likely a 5-10 solar mass dwarf.

source_title: The post-outburst photometric behaviour of V838 Mon
note: The light curve indicated that three eruptions took place in 2002 January, February and March.

source_title: The post-outburst photometric behaviour of V838 Mon
note: Echelle spectra indicated the ejection of a new shell of material shortly before the March maximum.

source_title: The post-outburst photometric behaviour of V838 Mon
note: Infrared excess in April 2002 indicated formation of a dust shell, associated with rapid optical fading in April-May.

source_title: The post-outburst photometric behaviour of V838 Mon
note: A simple shell-cooling estimate used a velocity of about 350 km/s, giving roughly 200 days for strong cooling evolution.

source_title: planets capture model of V838 Monocerotis: conclusions for the penetration depth of the planet(s)
note: Summary reports Rushton et al. (2003) upper limit of 0.01 solar masses for ejecta from absence of molecular emission.

source_title: planets capture model of V838 Monocerotis: conclusions for the penetration depth of the planet(s)
note: Summary reports Lynch et al. (2004) estimate of about 0.04 solar masses ejected, based on infrared observations and a spherical shell model.

time_phase: 2002 outburst total mass loss / literature compilation
source_title: planets capture model of V838 Monocerotis: conclusions for the penetration depth of the planet(s)
note: Summary reports Tylenda (2005) conclusion that total mass lost was about 0.001-0.6 solar masses; very uncertain.

source_title: planets capture model of V838 Monocerotis: conclusions for the penetration depth of the planet(s)
note: Summary reports Tylenda & Soker (2006) adopted an ejecta-mass range of 0.01-0.1 solar masses.

source_title: planets capture model of V838 Monocerotis: conclusions for the penetration depth of the planet(s)
note: Summary says Tylenda (2005) argued that the decline could be described by a collapsing envelope of about 0.2 solar masses.

source_title: V838 Mon: light echo evolution and distance estimate
note: The scattering material responsible for the light echo is probably a thin interstellar sheet rather than earlier ejecta from the star.
